primaryText: A CAT [...] caused himself [to] look as much as possible like a doctor...
translation: A CAT [...] caused himself [to] look as much as possible like a doctor...
comment: Attested (Aesop's Fables, Project Gutenberg), modified; [] marks insertion/deletion/replacement.

primaryText: A CAT [...] made himself look as much as possible like a doctor...
translation: A CAT [...] made himself look as much as possible like a doctor...
comment: The attested variant of the (3) triplet.

primaryText: A CAT [...] forced himself [to] look as much as possible like a doctor...
translation: A CAT [...] forced himself [to] look as much as possible like a doctor...
comment: Attested-modified; all three verbs acceptable in this frame.

primaryText: He caused cancer in one woman.
translation: He caused cancer in one woman.
comment: Attested (COCA SPOK: THE FIVE 5:00 PM EST; 2013).

primaryText: He made cancer [happen] in one woman.
translation: He made cancer [happen] in one woman.
comment: Starred: make does not substitute for cause in this frame.

primaryText: He forced cancer [to happen] in one woman.
translation: He forced cancer [to happen] in one woman.
comment: Starred: force does not substitute for cause in this frame.

primaryText: I caused Martha to go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
translation: I caused Martha to go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
comment: Mild causing event: only cause is acceptable.

primaryText: I made Martha go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
translation: I made Martha go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
comment: Starred.

primaryText: I forced Martha to go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
translation: I forced Martha to go to the gym by mentioning how the habit has helped me.
comment: Starred.

primaryText: I caused Martha to go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
translation: I caused Martha to go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
comment: Intermediate causing event: cause fine, make questionable, force out.

primaryText: I made Martha go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
translation: I made Martha go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
comment: Single-? diacritic in the paper.

primaryText: I forced Martha to go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
translation: I forced Martha to go to the gym by criticizing her physical appearance.
comment: Starred.

primaryText: I caused Martha to go to the gym by holding her child hostage.
translation: I caused Martha to go to the gym by holding her child hostage.
comment: Drastic causing event (removes safe alternatives): all three verbs acceptable.

primaryText: I made Martha go to the gym by holding her child hostage.
translation: I made Martha go to the gym by holding her child hostage.

primaryText: I forced Martha to go to the gym by holding her child hostage.
translation: I forced Martha to go to the gym by holding her child hostage.

primaryText: The child was made to get into the car, although she could've chosen to do otherwise.
translation: The child was made to get into the car, although she could've chosen to do otherwise.
comment: Halpern & Kleiman-Weiner: an action taken when the agent could not do otherwise is never intentional; make tolerates the could-have-done-otherwise continuation.

primaryText: The child was forced to get into the car, although she could've chosen to do otherwise.
translation: The child was forced to get into the car, although she could've chosen to do otherwise.
comment: Single-? diacritic: force resists the continuation asserting available alternatives.

primaryText: John caused the children to dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John caused the children to dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
comment: Paper's prose: easiest to imagine a situation where (9a) is true; no diacritics given on (9) — the judgment scale here transcribes the easiest/more difficult/most difficult ordering.

primaryText: John made the children dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John made the children dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
comment: Paper's prose: 'perhaps a bit more difficult to imagine'; diacritic assigned from prose ordering.

primaryText: John forced the children to dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John forced the children to dance, but he didn't intend for the children to dance.
comment: Paper's prose: 'most difficult to imagine'; diacritic assigned from prose ordering.

primaryText: John accidentally caused the children to dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John accidentally caused the children to dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.
comment: Hedging with accidentally improves the no-intent continuation for all three verbs.

primaryText: John accidentally made the children dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John accidentally made the children dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.

primaryText: John accidentally forced the children to dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.
translation: John accidentally forced the children to dance. He didn't intend for the children to dance.

primaryText: The pirate made the prisoner walk down the plank.
translation: The pirate made the prisoner walk down the plank.
comment: We can say with certainty that the prisoner walks down the plank — make conveys sufficiency of the causing action.

primaryText: The pirate let the prisoner walk down the plank.
translation: The pirate let the prisoner walk down the plank.
comment: The result is not guaranteed under let — the contrast motivating SUF as a graded measure.
